Basically, all you have to do to make millions of dollars in this gig is to write great copy and then make sure everybody knows about it, about your big wins and then be not that easy to hire. And that way they'll raise your prices. This has happened to me throughout my career where somebody would come to me and they'd say, okay, well, we want you to do this project and we're going to pay you $15,000 to write it. And I'd say, you know, guys, I just don't have the time. Okay, $20,000. You know? Okay, $25,000. Or even better with the royalties. I mean, I started out, my royalty rate used to be $10,000 in the mail and then it went to $20,000 and then it went to $50,000. So for every million pieces they mailed, I got $50,000. So the clients raised those rates. I didn't have to do it. So that's a key secret. And we talked yesterday about putting good marketing behind a bad product. I've done that with a client. I didn't know they were bad products, but they turned out to be. And it's had devastating consequences. In this case, you're the product. So you know, if you are, if you have your basic chops, you know how to do this. You understand the principles of direct response copywriting. You've taken the courses. You've read the masters, right? Then you're selling a great product. You're selling somebody that can do work miracles for your, for the clients. So this is the key. This is why I've given copywriting actually two full sessions at this weekend about building your business. So clients won't tell you this, but they really do want somebody, they really want more than just a copywriter. They want you to be a tactician and strategist. They want you to contribute ideas to the mix. They want you to do what Marcelo is doing to me right now, which is basically starting out writing a sales page for $12,000 or something, right? Plus royalties. And then making the pie bigger. So she's sometime today, I'm sure, probably tonight after I've had some wine, is going to tell me how much more this project's going to cost me. So you know, she came in and she made it bigger by saying, you know, we can make some good money if we just drive people to a sales page, but if we have this whole campaign built around it, where we educate them and diffuse their objections and handle all this stuff in advance, then you know, we can make a lot more money. And she's absolutely right. So what is it going to cost me? So you know, great technique to use with clients and it worked on me. You know, you'll have to send confidentiality and confidentiality clauses and some of your contracts. And that's just fine. This basically means that you don't reveal key information about this particular launch or project. But like Marcelo says, take everything you learn and run with it, you know? All right, so what are the things that would bring the most value to you as a copywriter right now? To me, the first one is product launch formula. And I've known just enough about it to be dangerous. But this is the direction that most of us online marketers are taking right now. And what it is is instead of having a one-shot deal where you have a sales page and you just say, hey, look at our sales page, you know, to your file or to an outside list even, you spend time doing several things. First of all, you're bonding with the file, okay? You're making friends because you're going to be providing them with free information, free tools, free things during this launch. You're diffusing their objections to the product before they even know there's a product. So you'll have editorial emails going to these people. And I'll give you an example. We just launched this tech stock service, right? So what's the biggest objection you could possibly have to investing in tech stocks? Well, the last time I bought tech stocks, you know, I got my head handed to me. The tech wreck in 2000, it took all my money back, all my winnings back and then some. So that's a big objection, right? What's another one? Well, they're complicated. I don't understand technology. How do I know what's a good company and what's a bad company? What's another objection? What's the timing? How do I know when, you know, when it's time to buy, when it's time to sell? So, you know, before we even hinted that there was going to be a product, I just stealthily started getting our daily e-zines, our daily online newsletters, to start answering these objections in articles. And I had the guy who was going to ultimately be my guru for the service do this in new articles. He was now bonding with the file. But I also had all my other editors. I said, okay, next two weeks, I want to see a lot of articles on how great tech technology stocks are right now. What a good deal they are. And so the file's just getting this messaging. And it's, they're almost getting it through osmosis at this point, right? And this went on for four weeks. It started in late February and it just ended in late March. That's what I'm talking about in terms of educating overcoming objections, right, and bonding with the file. Then you're building back pressure on the file, basically. I mean, during that period of time, each of those articles would say, hey, why don't you come on over to the blog and talk to us? You know, ask me your questions about tech stocks. And Markman, who's our guru, would be there on the blog and they'd actually be going back and forth with him. Hey, what do you think of Apple? What do you think of this? What do you think of that? And so they were starting to really understand and respect his expertise and bond with him. And then, of course, just to finish the thought on the campaign, after four or five weeks of that, we then had a series of podcasts. And they were just like, we call them podcasts. It's kind of inside baseball. But for them, it was, I want you to eavesdrop on a phone call I just had with Dr. Weiss. Or Martin would, Dr. Weiss would be saying, I want you, I just had this incredible call, phone call with John Markman. And I want you to be able to hear the recording. It's just amazing. And there was some drama. There was some tension. Martin is a perma bear. He's never seen a stock he likes. And here, John Markman's coming in, you know, and going, ah, you're nuts, man. We're making all this. I'm quadrupling my money on some of these stocks that I recommend. And Martin's going, ah, you know. But everyone got creamed last time. And then John answers that objection. And Martin goes, yeah, but you know, some of them don't have very much earnings. And John answers that. And so over another two week period, we're inviting people to listen to those calls. And again, more overcoming of objections. And then finally, at the end of that process, we said, OK, that's it, John. After this stuff, you need to name for me your favorite technology stocks now. And I want you to do it next Tuesday. And I want to invite anybody that wants to come. And so on a video event, you fly out here to Florida from Oregon or, yeah, Oregon. No, Washington. You fly out here from Washington. And we'll sit in the studio and we'll talk. And I want you to give me the megatrends that are driving the market now and the stocks that you're recommending now. And John goes, OK. So now we want to register people for that event, right? Because it creates a sense of obligation to actually attend that event. And so what I did was I said, well, we're going to present this event twice. We're going to present it once on Tuesday and another time on Wednesday. And what we're going to say is that, and we invited one million investors to attend this thing because we had some affiliate lists that were involved in this. And so I said, tell you what, because of the loyalty and friendship you've shown us here at Money Markets, I want to make sure that you get this information before we invite one million investors to see the recording of this event. So I want you to apply for a head of the line pass. And that entitles you to attend this event when presented on Tuesday. And then the very next day, one million investors are going to be invited to see the same thing. Everyone has a little sense of larceny in them. This is called front running. This is called inside information. If we had bought these stocks on Monday and then held the event on Tuesday and told everyone else to buy them, we'd be in jail right now. But it's OK for us, as long as we're not buying them, it's OK for us to, without really saying so, to just basically let the prospect draw its own conclusions. Let's see. If I attend on Tuesday at 2 o'clock, I still have several hours before the market closes and I can buy the stocks he recommends before one million investors. And what happens when those one million investors buy the stocks? So we appealed to their sense of kind of gentle legal larceny there. And we had a very good launch in terms of dollars in the very first day. Then I started thinking, normally at that point, what we would do is we would start driving traffic to the video of this event, driving all of that million people to the video of the event on Thursday, Friday, Saturday over the next two weeks, counting down to the end of the discount, the charter discount deadline. So then on Wednesday, though, I'm thinking, you know what? Why don't I have a repeat performance instead of driving them to a video? It's a subtle difference. But instead of doing what they're expecting, which is for me to tell them to go watch the video of something that happened days ago, why don't I say that this is a by popular demand, this is a repeat performance on Thursday. So that's what I did. And we got another nice, nice pop on Thursday. So that's my feeble, uninformed approach to product launch formula. Just based really on some time I spent on the phone with Jeff Walker, the guy who came up with this. I now have a full set of videos of his most recent product launch formula course. And I'm going to be going through that thing in great detail. But I mean, for me to spend hours and hours, and it is hours and hours, is probably, I don't know what, Paul, 50 videos, something like that? Or 30 maybe? I mean, each module has up to 15 videos in their six modules, right? So it gives you some indication, if I'm willing to give up that much of my time to learn something or learn how to do it better, how important this is. When you talk about what Marcella did at Motley Fool and what she's doing for me with our heat maps, that's all based on product launch formula. I was at the publishers roundtable. I think I'm making my point here, and I'll move on just a second. But Wendy and I attend the publishers roundtable every year. And what I did, I think I told you, is this incestuous group of all these financial publishers who get in a room and compete with each other to share the best idea that'll make you the most money. And Newsmax won this year. Guy named Aaron DeHogue, who's really a great guy. And he won with his idea about upsells on acquisition campaigns. But so we're at that thing. And Patricia, who runs marketing there at Motley Fool, she stands up and she goes up the front. She says, all right, well, a few years ago, I told you about this launch we did where we made $10 million off of this launch. And she goes, this year I'm going to tell you about a launch we did where we made $20 million. And it was a 10-week launch. And I think the theme was something like Shoot for the Stars. And it was this big, complex thing where you had to identify what your objectives were as an investor. It's illegal if you do it wrong, but they found a way to do it right. They played on that theme like crazy. For example, when you joined the service, you actually got the right to name a star. You can do that. So it was infotainment to the max. It was exciting. It was fun. It was engaging. It ran for, I think it was 10 weeks. And they walked away with $20 million. So that's how powerful Product Launch Formula is. That 10 million launch that they had done before was not Product Launch Formula. And so that's kind of the multiplier effect there. The other big trend that's going on right now is caused by these guys. These are the Gardner Brothers. They are the Motley Fools. And they come out of finance, not marketing. And so they have an aversion to long copy. And they've told Patricia and the team there at Motley, look guys, I don't want to see any 18-page sales pages. I don't want to see any two-page sales pages. I don't want to see any long copy of any kind. And that's forced Patricia into a mode where instead of presenting one long sales page, she presents a mini site that has seven or eight pages on it. So as a copywriter, you still write your long sales page. But then once you're done with a copy, you go back and you cut it into little chunks. And the first chunk is about John Markman. It's the bio of the guy. The next one is track record. The next one is five mega trends that you should invest in. The next one is five stocks that you should be looking at now. And it's all on a mini site. And then there's a one-page member benefits page. I tell you what, if any of you guys are not subscribed to Money in Markets, you need to do it, you'll get an email fairly quickly with a link in it inviting you to go see Markman's sales page. And you can see the website yourself and see how it's set up. So this is a big trend now. And it's working well because people's attention span online is very short. All the videos that are part of this are maybe five minutes long, maybe 10 at the most. And we have tracking software, so we watch the engagement. We can see a bunch of people bail out at one minute, then it'll be fairly flat, and then at five minutes, bang, people just leave. So we want the sweet spot in there where we have maximum viewership. We want to be able to present our best information in that period of time. The idea here is also good because your opening rates or your click-through rates on any one email you send out, if you're lucky, are going to be 20%. On some emails, depending on the day of the week, the subject line, and the sender line, you might be looking at a 2% click rate, which is very low, which means 98% of your file didn't see that message. So repetition is very important in these kinds of campaigns. So again, the three big changes that have happened recently, change number one has been going to campaigns instead of one-shot deals. Number two has been product launch formula, which I highly recommend you go to that website and buy the course. And the third has been the serialization of long copy. Long copy is still king, it still sells. It's just that part one may be one click away from part two and so forth. And our website, when you go there, this is the TechStock Superstars product, when you go to the website, you will find a sales page that I think it was about 18 pages long when I wrote it. So I still have that copy on there. But we've serialized that copy every way from sundown, in emails to the file on the site and so forth. So just be aware of those trends, get the skill if you possibly can. There are a lot of free videos online too. You can go to YouTube and type in product launch formula and probably get a lot of good information there as well. And this is so important that I've retained Paul Maxey, who is one of Jeff Walker's guys after Paul left my agency. And Paul's an expert in product launch formula. He's helping me with two launches right now. So it looks like I had slides for everything I just said and didn't go to them. Okay, again, to help you be a better copywriter, a more effective copywriter, get bigger winners more often. There are eight sales that you're going to have to make in your copy. You can turn this into a checklist. You can say, did I get my readers' attention? Did I get my prospects' attention? You've got to sell the guy on looking at your ad. You've got to stop him in his tracks and get him to look at your ad. Or a drug, mail piece or whatever. Secondly, and what does that? Your headline and lead, headline and deck, that stops him. One of my favorite headlines of all time was actually a national ampoune issue. And I had a picture of a dog sitting there looking all for Lorne and a human hand holding a gun to the dog's head. And the headline was, buy this magazine or we shoot the dog. So you understand all of this headline theory, and if you were boot camp, I gave you 37 ways to write a headline and everything. You understand the headline theory, but you understand that really all you're trying to do, you need to understand the purpose of the headline. And the purpose is to stop a guy in his tracks and make reading the deck copy or the opening copy the most important thing he could possibly do in the next minute. Then the readership sale, you've got to sell in your opening and so forth, you've got to sell people on continuing reading past just the first few paragraphs. And you need to make that sale over and over again throughout the piece. In a moment I'm going to share this, but first, a moment I'm going to give you that, but first let's look at this. The benefit sale, we talked about that yesterday, every practical and emotional benefit you can think of. The credibility sale, look at your copy and say, is there anything in here that just doesn't feel right, that I'm skeptical about, that I don't believe? The value sale, the way I said it yesterday was trivialize your price, just put it up there and laugh at it. Oh my gosh. Those guys that used to be on TV all the time at midnight that have appliance stores and crazy ads appliance store, everyone's yelling, stop him, he's crazy. Prices are too low. That's kind of what you want to be doing. You want to be going, gee, geez Louise, this guy has made his people four times their money in this service. That means you invest 10,000, you come away with 40,000. So it's $695 an insane price for this service, it's just ridiculous. And then you say, well, how many tanks of gas is that? Like divide it by 12, say how many tanks of gas is that a month or cups of Starbucks or whatever and compare it to something that the guy just thinks is trivial. The safety sale, that's a guarantee. We'll talk more about that in a minute. The convenience sale, you'll have to sell him on how convenient it is to order and the do it now sale. And in that convenience sale, I always include how fast the product will come and then the do it now sale. There's got to be a reason to do it immediately right now, not tonight, not in 10 minutes because once he's off your page or once he's set down your mega log or whatever, you've lost him. Now we've talked about practical and emotional benefits. I just wanted to do this slide to kind of help you through it a little bit. What's your real product? There's a copywriter in this room who wrote a headline once. Flush deadly toxins out of your colon. How many of you woke up in the middle of the night and said, oh my God, I forgot to flush deadly toxins out of your... I think the piece worked. I don't know if you changed the headline after I credited it. Yeah, you hated it. But we did test it and it did work but then there was another one that was wrong. It did work better, yeah. So you need to connect with your prospect really where he is. So let's look at kind of the benefit chain on a product. This is actually a real product, oral chelation. What's a feature? Well it contains EDTA. That's this chemical that has been shown to reverse heart disease. It actually dissolves plaque inside the arteries. So the functional benefit is it removes plaque from your arteries. The practical benefit is that it lowers your risk of a heart attack. You see, removes plaque from your arteries really isn't the benefit is it. It's like a step towards the benefit. But the real benefit is it lowers your risk of heart attack. So even if we had just gone with that throughout the piece, this product lowers your risk of a heart attack. We would have probably done okay with it but we took the next step to make it an emotional benefit. It ends heart nightmares for good. Anybody who's older has palpitations, they have high blood pressure, they worry about their heart. I just started doing that last week as a matter of fact. So the appealing to that fear got this piece read. So you can see the progression there. Any questions about that? Another capils list. I don't have to go through these. You do have this slide, right? Or no? All right, well it'll be in your new set when you download your new set. But this is a great checklist. You can look at your headline and see how well it does these things. Oh that was a timed dissolve. That's weird. I hope this isn't auto. We'll deal with it. So use this checklist, look at your headline and say does it do these things and if not, why not? And how can I make it do these things? Just a lot of really good idea starters here. Another tip, go through your piece, your copy and say is there enough entertainment to make this fun to read, to make people want to continue listening or viewing or reading? Is there intrigue? Is there a mystery to be solved? Is there some irony? You know it's weird. There are two superstitions I have that are completely unproven. The first one is if the first words of your sales copy are a date, any kind of date. On Tuesday, February 3rd, 1963, such and such happened. Or forward looking data. Sometime in the future something is going to happen on a particular date. I've never had a piece of copy that started with a date not win. I mean it's just weird. The other one is the use of the word ironic in a headline or a debt copy. In the best case and in the worst case, presenting an irony without calling it that. But there's something about irony that intrigues us. We just love these juxtaposed ideas or these competing ideas that seem somehow to live together. Drama. I was looking at some copy this morning about, it was a headline debt copy and opening copy, a relationship counselor. How he could get women's husbands to actually listen to them. He can do that. There are a lot of women who are feeling emotionally strangled and starved and they're not getting the attention from their significant other that they want. The headline that I suggested was basically to put that into the mouth of a woman who had gone through this guy's therapy. Have her state. I would have never believed in a million years that old Fred over there could have ever become emotionally available and a great lover and all that stuff. Drama. Tell a little bit, oh Fred did all these horrible things or didn't do all these good things and now he spent 10 minutes with his relationship counselor and all of a sudden my God, the man is a Casanova. And then the letter, now it presents a problem doesn't it? If you have the woman saying that in the headline and deck, how do you change the voice of the letter suddenly? Because it's got to be from the relationship counselor, right? So very simple. Usually it's a Gordian knot solution, the simplest solution in the past. And it's just, you know, dearest so and so. When the woman, Susan, let's say, she identifies herself as when Susan first came to me, her marriage was a mess. That one line does a transition, now we're hearing from the expert who worked this miracle, right? You can see how emotionally engaging that is. So anyway, controversy is another one, it's really great. I love creating controversy. That's why God invented blogs. Bob Bly and I had a little conspiracy years ago when I was first starting the total package. And we said, let's do an interview. And he wanted to introduce me to his file. And so we did this interview. And during the interview I said something about headlines. I forget what I actually said. But anyway, he then tells his entire file that Clayton Makepeace is engaging in heresy saying that headlines don't matter. You've got to hear this interview. Is the man insane? I said nothing like that. But he just created this little tempest in a teapot and we probably got twice as many people to listen to that interview and subsequently sign up to the total package because of it. I thought he was pretty brilliant. And we actually never had the conversation. I just saw him doing it and immediately got it. It was like two marketers going, oh, okay. See where this is going? Yeah. So really good. Okay. You can use short copy for most of your emails, definitely for most of your videos. If we had been talking this time last year or two years ago, I would have said one hour video sales pages. And I did say in my seminar last year, I think, one hour video sales pages are just fine. Now that's changed. And I think a series of 5, 10, maybe 15 minute installments on the video sales page is the way most clients would prefer to go. Squeeze pages. I learned a valuable lesson here. You think Marcella talks a lot. My copy is long, long, long, long. And so I would write this masterpiece of a squeeze page. Does anyone not know what a squeeze page is? Squeeze pages. We cover that. Okay. If I'm trying to produce leads online, not people to actually buy yet, but just leads, right? I'll drive them to a squeeze page, offering them a free report. And on that squeeze page, it asks them for their name and email address in order to get the report. And there's some legal language that means now it's okay for me to market to them. So that's a squeeze page. So I wrote this epic squeeze page for one of our products. And it just wasn't working. And so we brought in this group that specializes in online acquisition. They're really a media company, but they looked at the squeeze page and they said, let's try this. And they came up with this thing. It had a picture. It had one paragraph of copy in the places where you could put your stuff in. So people didn't have to feel like they had to read this thing and then do it. It was just like right there, just put in your name and you're done. So short video squeeze pages. And the same thing with download pages. A download page is after you've offered somebody, this is for acquisition, not for your customer file. After you've offered somebody a free report, they've gone through the squeeze page in order to get it. They're taken to a download page. And once again, download page. You want them to get the report. What's in it for you? Well, first of all, they'll see how smart your guru is because it's a great report. Secondly, probably at the end of the report, there's a link that will take them to a sales page where they could buy the guru's product. So you definitely want them to download it. So once again, picture the thing. Here's your download. Just click this button. Very, very short copy. I talked about this earlier. These are some of the pages I'm using now on my mini site for tech stock superstars. Now as you're working with a client on a particular product, a guy comes to you and says, I want you to sell my ICHOT. So the governing principle here is that your readership rate on any email you send out could be as low as 10%, 5%. The number of people don't put too much stock in open rates. You guys get an email. How many of you double-click the email to open the pane to read the email in its own window as opposed to reading it in the preview pane on your outlook or whatever? How many of you actually open the email? Three? Five out of 30. I never open emails. I never open. I read them in the preview pane. So open rates are very deceiving. They're only significant in context. So if you want to look back over time and say that my open rates have traditionally been this and all of a sudden we have this massive shift, then it, you know, then they make sense. But it really matters what I really watch is click through rates. So people who read the email and click the link in the email. So 5% to 10% on some emails, 15% to 20% on other emails depending on the context and everything else that's going on, subject line sender. So it's really important to use all the media at your disposal. So emails, blog postings, we talk about that, get a controversy going. On total package, I used to love making political comments and that every once in a while. Here I am doing a marketing blog, right? All about copywriting and stuff. And I would say something about, I referred to the president, the speaker of the house, and Harry Reid as the unholy trinity or father, son, and holy cow. Now there are two things happen because of that, okay? The first thing that happened was, whereas I was used to getting 20 or 30 people getting on my blog and discussing the article that I just did, I had like 500. And they were yelling and throwing things and it was great. But the second thing that happened was I got audited for the first time in my life. Within two weeks, I got the letter from the IRS. So, but anyway, blog posts are great for that, for engagement. Social media, we talked about yesterday. Easy articles, special reports. I told you how Rich Sheffrin built his whole company around that marketing, internet marketing manifesto. I want to talk to you about the jury system. I think I'm going to find me on the page. Oh, good. Already taken the advice. Surveys are great. You can ask all kinds of great questions. You can solicit multiple choice answers or open-ended answers. Podcasts, again, this could be the telephone call that you could be a fly on the wall while Dr. Weiss talks with Ron Paul. I didn't know that was going to rhyme, but you can listen in as Art Laffer talks to Mike Larson. There's something kind of, again, a little naughty about that. It's voyeuristic, so it's very attractive. The video online launch event, one of the things that we found works really well, just mentioned Art Laffer and Ron Paul, is that we go to speakers' bureaus and we find somebody that's a specialist in that particular area. A couple of years ago, for example, we were launching a service on investing in oil stocks. So we got T. Boone Pickens to come and sit with Martin in the studio and talk about the profit potential in oil. We just did a launch with Charles Goyette that had to do with fighting inflation, preserving your assets against inflation. So we hired Ron Paul and he came to town and we had a great time with him. He's a really cool dude. He sat in the studio with Martin and they talked about the impact of inflation and how you should defend yourself. It's expensive, but if you're working with Motley Fool, they don't care that it's $50,000 to hire Ron Paul. If it's a justified expense in their mind in terms of getting viewership and so forth. There's something to think about. Our video launch events tend to have more than one person in them, but they don't have to. Let me give you another example here of a serialized video sales page. We launched a service last year called Golden Silver Trader and the expert that was driving that service is very popular on our file. His name is Larry Edelson. He's got 75% approval rating. So I thought, well, this is all about bonding, education, and overcoming objections. So it would be the best way to do that. So I created a six-module course on gold investing, gold and silver investing. I had Larry teach it and I outlined each module. He said, here's what I want you to cover in each module. So now this is basically a sales page, but presented as a course value added. Normally this would be $1,800, but I want you to have it free because gold and silver are bottoming now and it's important you have this information. So we'd present one module every third day in between. We'd drive people back to the video of that module in preparation of the presentation of the next module and so forth. At the end of each module, there was a very soft sell. It was just, I've just launched my new service, Gold Silver Trader, if you want details on it, click this link. And we did $6.5 million on that one. There was no hyperbole. There was no big promises. We did talk about how much gold and silver stocks had risen in the recent past. And some of them were pretty spectacular. But there were no over the top claims or promises at all. So as you're thinking through a campaign with your client, use this checklist and go, okay, are we using emails, blog posts? How could each one of these things fit into the overall? We've made this point several times. We have repeatedly made the point about repetition. This goes along with this whole new approach to copywriting. It's relationship marketing online now. It's relationships. Now, if somebody ran up to you on the street and started screaming, shrink hemorrhoids in three minutes or less, you'd grab your purse and run like hell. So it's getting to be the same way online. It's getting to be the point where you make a friend first, then you make a customer. And so the tone tends to be very warm instead of very hyperbolic. One of the best things you can do for credibility is to point out the elephant in the room. Say you're talking about tech stocks, like I made the point earlier, talking about tech stocks. What you can think of is a tech rack. Tech stocks fell, I forget what it was, some ridiculous amount, 60%, 70%. And so many of them were totally wiped out. So that's what a lot of people are going to be thinking. So you can even have the line, I know what you're thinking. You're thinking that, and you were heard in the tech rack. You bought tech stocks like everybody else, but you were heard in the tech rack. So think about this. Is your barber giving you tech stock recommendations today? No. But he was then, how about your taxi driver? How about your brother-in-law? Are people just running around in a panic trying to find the next great tech stock? No. What does that mean? It means they're fairly priced and they're nowhere near a bubble. But still, they're going up like crazy and here are some examples. So that's really called personal proof, right? Because you've reasoned with him and taken him through this objection. But just simply by being the first guy to raise the issue and saying, I know what you're thinking, your credibility just skyrocketed. This guy is not trying to dazzle me with bullshit. He's real. We talked about this, whether campaign or in a single sales page, very important to do this. Again, talk with your prospect, not at him. We don't want Billy Sunday preachers here, Kari Nation preachers. We want people who will come alongside and be a friend and be a partner and be a guide for the customer. We talked about this again yesterday, but it's very important. So I have it repeated here with a little bit of detail. Time-limited guarantees are great. You have to make this purchase by the end of the day or by the end of the hour. One company, Prosper Learning, tell you, if you want to see some really good marketing, look up Prosper Learning online. They have a really good funnel and you'll like what you see. They did the test and found that ten minutes was a magic number. So you have to accept this offer in the next ten minutes. Shorter times didn't work as well, longer times didn't work as well. Quantity available, quantity at price. Another, there's only this many available at this price. There's only this many available, period. Something I did a lot with Rodale was something we called the Fast 50. So it's only the first 50 people who respond will get this price. Now, I don't understand the back office or the compliance ramifications of that. But I'm like 100% sure that they didn't reject the 51st order. Yeah, and I think the FTC doesn't really have a problem with that because, I mean, what you're doing is good for the consumer, not bad, right? So, but again, the compliance people will sort that out for you. Your guarantee should be shocking, in some way or surprising. Most of us just sleepwalk through the guarantee. Think about making it my personal contract with you. I promise that I will do these things. If I don't do these things, I don't want to keep a penny of your money. Just let me know at any time within this time period and I'll rush you a refund. It should be, it could be a contract like that, a solemn promise. It could be performance based. I've used a performance based guarantee with Safe Money Report now since 1999. And it's that we will make you or save you at least $5,000 in the next 12 months. Or just at the end of 12 months, let us know and we'll give you a full refund. So basically, we'll give you $5,000 or Safe Money Reports free. How can you lose, right? Something else about guarantees, keep in mind, okay? We did some really good tests on guaranteed length. And what we found out was, the longer the guarantee period, the fewer refund requests you'll get. And the reason for that is, it should be obvious. It is to me, it's not to our CEO and sometimes I just get so frustrated. But it's obvious. If I tell you you've got 30 days to make your final decision and ask for a refund or not, you're gonna have a posted on your refrigerator. And especially if it's a high price service. If you just paid $5,000 for this service, right? And you can get that five grand back after a month or 60 days or 90 days. You're gonna have, there's gonna be a red mark on your calendar for the date that you have to, we're basically offering you $5,000 to cancel at that point. So that kind of guarantee is really turning against you. It's a very bad way to go. Yes? As far as the guarantees, when you're sort of scouting out your clients, that's when the question is you need to ask your clients, what is your guarantee? Or is it testable? Yeah, or just because you'll learn about the client from that comment. If that client is not willing to give 100% money back guarantee, don't work with them. They don't trust their product. They don't trust you, they don't trust their business because if they can't stand behind the product, then they shouldn't be in business at all. I just did a test, not a test, but pulled together a bunch of information for a client that's out of the country because he wanted to know what the US guarantee policies were. So we picked the top 30 health marketers and went through all of their guarantee process. One thing that I found interesting doing that little test was the clients that were struggling the most in the market had the shortest guarantee. They were offering 30 day money back. Some of the guarantees were ridiculous. You have to send us back everything wrapped up, mailed, they sort of include your first born with it. Send us back the unopened package. Yeah, it's just when I send us back the unopened package. So anyway, that's something that except with me and even earlier in my career, that was one thing that I had learned, Clayton actually taught me, was that you'll learn a lot about the client just by what they will to stand behind. Yeah, absolutely. The client who doesn't stand behind his product, it could be three things. It could be he doesn't believe in the product. It could be he's unbelievably cheap, and this is pennywise pound foolish cheap. Or it could be that his CFO is telling him that this creates enormous unfunded liabilities on the list. Because at the end of the year, when they count up their money and try to declare a profit or whatever, they have to deduct the rest of the subscriptions that they have to fulfill. So there's a complicated accounting reason there, so a tax reason. So, but we actually did the test. We tested all 30 days, 90 days, six months, one year, one year one. Now last year, it was hilarious. We promoted this service called All Weather Investor, and I wrote the copy with a one year guarantee. And the price was $1,100 for the service for one year. And the guy had never had a losing quarter in his life. Since we promoted that service, he hasn't had a single winning quarter. Okay? So month after month after month, customer service is saying, everybody has marked their calendars for the end of the year. Everybody's gonna cancel. We had to set aside $1.5 million to fund cancels. That's a million and a half I didn't get paid a bonus on, by the way. So we set aside for all this huge amount of cancels that we're gonna be coming in. I kept telling people, you're deluded, it's not gonna happen. If people want canceled, they'll cancel in the second quarter, third quarter, their subscription. They won't necessarily wait to the end, especially if they're losing money. You're not gonna have a flood of cancels. Well, guess what? We didn't have a flood of cancels. It didn't happen. So I'm about to get a second bonus on a million and a half that is being restored to our bottom line. But the customer service people, everybody just had their panties in a knot about this. I mean, the customer service guys were going, so-and-so called in and said they're thinking about canceling and stuff like that. So it's a good principle to keep in mind. The longer the guarantee, the lower your refund rate is likely to be. Use low key friendly, we talked about this. It's now a relationship business. It's not a hyperbole business. By the way, does anyone know what hyperbole means? What's the definition of hyperbole? Yeah, hype is short for hyperbole. Yeah, so what's hype? Okay, let me tell you what it really is. A client will say to you, this is so hypey. He has no idea what he's talking about. Hyperbole is the intentional exaggeration in order to make a point. Intentional obvious. I'm so hungry I could eat a horse. Okay, that's hyperbole. Okay, so when a client tells you that this has hype in it or it's hypey, he doesn't know what hype is. He doesn't know what hyperbole is. And I look, cuz what a vague crit that is, right? So taking a task for it, tell me, come on, be specific. All right, so make a friend first then make a sale. These are qualities that your copy should have. There should be some reciprocity, okay? You can get that in a lot of different ways. Giving away something free in the copy is one way to do it. Getting people to register for an event or to read a report or a sales page. You've reserved a seat for them, how rude if they don't show up, right? And you can find nice ways to say that in the copy. We're gonna have to give your seat away to someone else. If you don't register, and once they're registered, you say, we've reserved a seat for you. That means that there are some people that aren't getting in because you got this seat. So create an obligation, likeability, again, make a friend first, authority. Demonstrating expertise is really a huge key to what we do. And the people who are hitting all these homeruns for boardroom, they're doing it with these packages that do what I told you about where they go through a book and all of the proof elements or all of the tips in the book that are like, wow, I would have never thought of that. Those are sidebars. So they're actually demonstrating the expertise of the people who wrote the book, right? David, I wanted to say this the other day, but David is a master when he acknowledged masters of fascination writing. And he had this book he was trying to promote. And there was this tip in the book that said, if you have a sore back, try taking a wallet out of your hip pocket, because sitting on that wallet all day can throw your back out of kilter and give you sore back. So David wrote, how a pick pocket can cure a backache? I still rate, we were in the men's room yesterday and I'm still laughing about that fascination. I thought it was so great. So social proof is the next one and that's the testimonials we talked about that. Success stories, all kinds of vignettes, case histories and so forth. Personal proof is the reasoning with people in the scarcity that we've talked about several times too. These are the new power words. Forget that book Wendy and I published. Well, it was done by Anthony, right? Emotional trade. Yeah, yeah. Lots of creative emotional words you can use when you're happy. Okay, well anyway, these are the ones. You master these. I love it. I love that book. A lot of people do. A lot of people do, a lot of people do. But to be fair, there's a lot of good idea starters in there. But this is what people are responding to now. You knew free save because instantly, okay? Telling something that they can download a report and is one thing saying you can instantly download a report is gonna get you more clicks. Save, sale, you save all those items. They've been selling everything from washing machines to magic wand since the beginning of time. Instantly, because when you make a statement, a lot of times you need to explain why you said that. Okay? Or for example, the buying power of the dollar is gonna crash. Because the Federal Reserve has been printing money like there's not tomorrow. Give plenty of rationale for your statements or for the claims that you make in the copy. These are some actual tests. Explain in your email why the recipient was selected to receive this message. I'm writing you today because you remember this or you subscribed to that or because I know that you're interested in this or that at 15% lift. Adding the word small to the word fee. Your membership is $39 a year or saying, the memberships are available for the small fee of whatever. I gave a 20% lift. Told any involvement, this was in a fundraising piece, but any involvement, level of involvement will help. All of a sudden, when you're planning to donate to somebody, campaign or charity or something, you want your donation to count. One of the things that they're counting on is that you're gonna give, excuse me, give enough so that the people in the organization will take note of your contribution. And the donor is aware of this. He's feeling that pressure as he's trying to pick out his dollar amount. So they relieve the pressure by saying any involvement will help. And if you're prospecting as a fundraising organization, what you're really interested in is getting the donor. So it removed the barrier to entry. So I thought that was really cool. You could use this rationale even on a newsletter, where you tell them, look, whether you take six months a year or two years, any level of membership will help you achieve your goal. We did this, this is the theme for the campaign that we're running now, working like gangbusters. We talked about mantras and themes yesterday, but I just wanted to reinforce that. It's wonderful. I mean, Motley Fool's thing about Reach for the Stars was great. Dreaming big about tech stocks, all of a sudden now I'm not thinking about the crash. I'm thinking about all that excitement that led up to the crash and all the money I made. And the only problem was I didn't get my money off the table fast enough. And this time around you can, cuz you know what's gonna happen, right? Eventually every bull market ends. So prove every point, third party sources, media endorsements, expert endorsements, case histories, customer testimonials. Create a slippery slope. Always look for ways to reduce friction. We call friction anything that slows down the ordering process. We talked about the bread crumb sign up yesterday, where they start this buying process by just giving you their first name. And then, yes, their last name, yes, or actually the first one is the email address. So they're not confronted with that big order form, right? That's reducing friction. The short squeeze page instead of the long one is reducing friction. The five minute video instead of the 15 minute or the one hour video, reducing friction. It's all about making more of this an impulse sale for the prospect. I like transactional too. I like transactional appeals. The probably the best headline I ever wrote, you wouldn't even recognize as a headline. It was give me 90 days. And I'll help you, dot, dot, dot. And it was a picture of a doctor saying it. And then underneath there were three things. It was like reverse heart disease, block cancer, you know, maybe one of the days you could actually say that stuff. And so it was transactional. Somehow, things are more credible if I'm asked to give something up, okay? So I was asking you to give up 90 days just to try these things. And I'll help you do these things. And of course, I tied that into my guarantee in the copy. Makes it very hard not to accept it. We talked about this yesterday. Now I got 37 question acid test for sales copy. We're coming to the end of our time. This is also not in your book, I don't think. But it will be in your other thing. Let's just pick out a few of the high points here. The second bullet, did the headline and lead instantly seize your attention? Okay? Were they instantly believable? You know, there's this technique in writing copy that I think is very important. And that is to get your prospect doing this as early as you can in the text. So you present some mutually agreed upon point. And it can be as benign as the weather. It could be just something, get him going, uh-huh, uh-huh. So now instead of, think about when you go into car dealership, right? And you go into the salesman's office. These people are not very bright in this regard. Who's gonna sit where? Well, he's gonna sit behind the desk, which is a position of authority. And you're gonna be sitting in front of his desk in a very uncomfortable chair. Right? And they think that's a good idea. And I think maybe in the old world it was. But what is that? That's confrontation and it's intimidation. Okay? Now with the internet, I don't have to buy it from the sky. I can get online and get a better price. I just, I bought it, my Shelby, I bought it up in North Carolina and had it shipped down from Charlotte. You know? And I had to search the entire country before I found the thing. So I bought it online, basically. So what's a better solution? Well, a better solution would be for the two of you to be sitting side by side. Now instead of this confrontational thing, this arm wrestling contest, right? Now you've got a helper who's helping you to get what you want. It's a whole different psychology. And you know, this is one of the ways, human nature never changes. So when you get your copywriting chops, it'll stay with you forever. But sometimes the way that we communicate with each other change. Okay? And this is one of those ways. So keep that in mind. Always want to be the champion for the prospect, not the adversary. Again, explaining why it's crucial you read this right now. The readership ride. Very, very important. Keep in mind that chain of events that you set off when you put your headline out there. They read the headline and go, oh yeah, really? So I think I'll read the deck. The next, the paragraphs of bold copy before the salutation. So they read that and go, oh, okay, that makes sense. Probably would be a good idea to read some more. Get a little more information here. So they read the first few paragraphs of the letter. Every paragraph in that point on has to sell me on reading the next paragraph. There's one of the reasons why using connecting words at the beginning of paragraphs is so important. Or phrases in addition, plus in the meantime. You know, even starting paragraphs with the word and. It says, you know, to really get the good out of what you just read, you really need to move on to the next paragraph. So be in touch with how you're feeling as you're reading through the copy. And ask other people to read the copy and be in touch with how their feeling as they're moving through it. It's, remember, we buy for emotional reasons, not for practical ones. Spokesman should always give a reason why he wrote this, why he's communicating with the prospect right now. You know, what's his reason? What's in it for him? And it's not obviously to sell a product. You know, it's because his father had a heart attack and he just would hate to see that happen to another family. You know, or some other reason that implies passion and concern for the prospect. This is interesting because these are taken from capels. Look at this one. Does a copy feel like a one-on-one conversation between two friends? He probably wrote this in the 40s, you know, or 50s maybe. But, you know, it's so pertinent for today. Does a spokesman have an emotional stake? Oh, yeah, you guys have heard me say this before. I first heard it from Dan Kennedy and I thought it was hilarious. He said, you know, if you're sitting in your living room looking out the window and you see your neighbor's roof is on fire and you casually walk across the street and you knock on his door and he comes a door and you say, excuse me, sir, but your roof seems to be oxidizing fairly rapidly. He's gonna, you know, what? But if you go yelling and screaming across the street, pound on his door, don't wait for him to open it, you open the door, you're a roof's on fire. You know, you'll get the appropriate response, right? So the emotion in your copy needs to be appropriate for the message, right? So are there times when you need to be excited? Yes. Are there times when you need to be angry? Yes. Are there times when you need to be this vociferous champion for your prospect? Yes. And those can be highly emotional times and they can use emotional power words, you know. But it needs to be appropriate for the message that you're conveying at the time. A lot of people, you know, are good at focusing on the positive things that a product will do or the positive emotions it'll trigger. Think about the negative emotions that not having the product will trigger. Get that into the copy. Remember that headline, How to Cancel Your Next Heart Attack? The heart attack that didn't have to happen or didn't happen? Those immediately invoke this or address the fear that people have about the subject. Rationale. No, I don't do this enough. Rationale for discounts and premiums. You know, I am really guilty of this one. You know, it's great when you can say in there, I want you to have this because. It's important that you get this. I created this for you because, you know, whether it's a premium or actually the product. Very important. Prospects desire for instant gratification addressed. At Health Resources, we had the most involved order form you've ever seen in your life, the circulation. It was a full page of a tabloid. It had testimonial and the facing page. We had testimonials, success stories, guarantees represented graphically as well as in the text. We had how to order, one, two, three. I mean, this thing was the most involved thing you ever saw in your life. But what it did was all by itself it could have sold the product. And one of the things that we did with that was that we said, we got the client to agree to guarantee that the product would be shipped within 24 hours. So it addressed everyone wants instant gratification, right? So it addressed that, you know, that you're going to get this quickly. Let's see. Order form, copy, just covered that. Easy use. Okay, and then, of course, remember that this order, if it's from an outside list, is the beginning of a relationship with this customer. So don't stop at the order form. You know, talk to your client and say, hey, could I write the thank you letter? You know, could I start that bonding process for you with the customer? There's some things I'd like to try there. If it's a sale to a house file guy, what I'd love to do, and what we're going to do on this product we're promoting now, is as soon as they buy, you give them something free, you know, that they're not expecting, the unexpected premium. And that, you know, when that happens, you're just bonded to this guy. At this point, he can make a lot of mistakes and you're going to sit, you know, those mistakes out with him. So, a lot to think about here. When you get these slides, turn these into a checklist as well. I think it will really help your copy. All right, let's take a break. When we come back, we'll get back to doing deals with new customers, new clients.
